transcript.text: How are you?
Doing well.
How's your day going?
It's pretty good.
Remind me where you're located.
I am actually in Iowa.
Oh, cool.
Nice.
Yeah.
Middle of the country.
Yeah.
I'm in New York.
It's going good here.
We're having our quarterly business reviews for the sales team here.
So we've got a bunch of people here in the New York office today, which is fun.
Nice.
That was a good time to have a team get together.
Yeah.
Well, wonderful.
So today, I just really wanted to go a little bit deeper on the products.
So we've got a little bit of context from the team during kickoff, kind of aligning on audience and goals.
And so we really kind of wanted to just connect, you know, the Hex product, especially the evolution you guys have gone through recently.
Thank you.
We kind of really understand what makes Hex truly AI native.
So any proof points or workflows that show that best.
I'm not sure if there's a deck that you have that you typically go through.
Otherwise, I've got several questions I can just run through.
Yeah, I'm happy to answer your questions.

The way I was thinking of doing this, and tell me if this makes sense for you, is I have a few slides that I kind of run through with the team, like when we have like a new person on the team, we're kind of like talking through like how do we position the technology and like just to give them a sense like the terms and definitions we use and kind of how we frame the problem.
And then I thought I could like go into the product and like kind of show you a demo of like how that comes to life in the product.
Is that work for you?
That's amazing, yeah.
Okay.
Do you just interrupt me as we go?
If one of your questions is like, if it's the right time to answer one of those, I'm happy to.
Okay.
I've just made it a conversation.
So this is the deck that I use for new people on the team to kind of talk through how we tell our story.
And I'm just going to show you a couple of the slides here.
We're a fairly technical product.
And I think some of the terms we use maybe are specific to us.
So it might be useful just to kind of see that first.
So the way I'm kind of framing, like, the problem we solve right now is, like, increasingly people are having experiences with technology that look like this.
Like, across every sector, like, people are seeing more and more that, like, technology is evolving.
They can just, like, type in a prompt, type in a question, and get an answer.
But when it comes to working with data, when people, like, you know, every organization has, like, lots and lots of data that they know.
they're...
So they're...
There's like insights hidden in that can help them make decisions.

But when it comes to like asking those types of simple questions from data, they have this really complex convoluted experience where like maybe they ask a question, they get sent to like a library where there's a bunch of reports and dashboards.
Maybe one of those answers, one of their questions, maybe it doesn't.
They get a license to a tool where they can kind of explore the data themselves.
Maybe that's too technical for them to use.
They ask a data practitioner for help.
They get told they'll get an answer back in two weeks.
And maybe they just don't end up using data at all.
Maybe they end up like doing something in an Excel spreadsheet.
It's just very, it's far removed from like this experience that I think people are starting to expect to have from technology.
So like that's kind of the gap we're seeing.
And like the way we would describe the reason that data hasn't been able to like keep up with this technical change is the tools like haven't evolved to like be AI native.
So like a lot of data.
Practitioners still have like a lot of different tools.
They might use like one tool for querying data, another tool for doing data science, a third tool for doing like reporting, a fourth tool for spreadsheets.
That makes it very difficult to like piece together one question when you have to kind of jump between all these different tools.
So that's one of the problems we solve.
Another is there we find the data teams are kind of consistently like being asked to answer like quick questions from business stakeholders.
Like they, you know, people in the business maybe don't have technical knowledge.
They need help answering questions and it just becomes they become inundated with these like one off questions.
And it's very difficult for them to like prioritize and figure out where they should be spending their time.
And then there are AI tools out there, but the AI tools that have been delivered kind of so far have been kind of kind of point solution.
They don't solve the whole problem.
Maybe they're like a chat bot that kind of helps them.
Query the data, but don't help them visualize it, or something like that.
So those are kind of like the problems we see right now.
Usually when we're talking to like a data practitioner or data scientist, they're kind of nodding along and being like, yeah, I have these problems too.
In terms of like how Hex solves that, we solve it by bringing all data workflows together in one platform that has AI integrated throughout.
So we've got tools for deep data work.
So we've got notebooks, which I'll show you in the product demo.
That's kind of where you do like deeper data science, like a person that's like pretty technical, really sophisticated with data, can do very complex analysis in our notebooks.
Those notebooks can then be published as data apps.
Those are kind of like low-code apps where people can, you know, non-technical people can see the answers that were found by the data person.
And then we also have self-service capabilities, which are for those non-technical people.
Well, like ask their own questions.
Maybe the answer to their question wasn't in the app.
They want to ask their own question.
They've got AI tools that help them explore data on their own.
So that's a brief summary of kind of like what problem we think we solve and what the product does.
I'll go ahead and flip over to Hex, and I'll kind of show you how that comes to life.
You should be able to see my screen now.
Yeah, I think it's coming up.
Cool.
So this is what Hex looks like, and I'm going to start actually with that last use, as I mentioned, of like self-service.
So like imagine I'm a business person.
I have a quick question of data.
I want to be able to answer it.
I'm not like a super technical person.
You know, maybe it's, you know, who are our top performing sales reps?
This capability that I'm showing here is called Threads.
And this uses AI to allow that non-technical person to type in their question, natural language, and then get a pretty detailed analysis that AI is helping generate for us.
And there's a couple kind of cool things that we do here.
The biggest concern that customers have right now with AI tools for data is, like, do they give you, like, trustworthy results?
You know, AI can hallucinate when you ask it to do math.
If you go to, like, chat GPT and ask a question, it doesn't have, like, the context of your business's data.
So we've built a bunch of tools in Hex to make it so we can deliver, like, more trusted, accurate results.
So you can see as it's working, it's, like, it's looking through existing projects and being like, oh, are there existing analysis in your organization that help answer this question?
Are there existing data models?
So one of the things you can do in Hex is you can model data to kind of give instructions to the AI.
So for example, like it found like closed one conversion rate is an interesting thing to use to answer my question of who are our top reps.
And there's a certain way my organization defines that metric and it can use that rather than like the business user having to know how to do that sort of computation.
And then it kind of deeply thinks to the question and gives me this report that has like, here's the top sales reps by closed one ARR.
Here's a table that shows, you know, the number of opportunities they're winning and the average per opportunity.
And there's even some kind of natural language explanations of like kind of the reasoning it went through.
And it also found an app like, oh, here's another analysis that already exists that you might want to use to kind of like help answer that question.

I think another issue that we often see customers ask about with like reporting tools and things like that is like there can be a lot of different copies of the same analysis, like five different people have asked the same question.

So as much as possible, we're like trying to point people to existing answers so that we can kind of use those answers as like context to kind of help everyone come to a consensus on what the answers are.
So this obviously isn't like an instantaneous thing.
We've kind of like built it for deep reasoning.
It's kind of like using chat GPT and like deep thinking mode where it goes through the steps in a lot of detail to produce like a thoughtful analysis.
Something that like maybe you'd ask like a junior analyst to do in like the pre-AI world.
And you can continue to like have a conversation like I'd ask a follow-up question and be like, oh, you know, is there differences per segment or whatever?
And kind of continue the conversation.
But the other thing that Hex does that's really cool is all of these threads are also instantly transferable into Hex's data science notebooks.
So I just clicked a button and I opened it as a notebook.
And now this is the environment that Hex uses for like deeper kind of data science work.
So you can see it's got like a markdown of what the thread found for us.
And it's got any like queries it ran.
It's got the visualizations in more of like an editing mode.
So if I'm like trying to build a report, I can kind of go from there.
And the AI agent is here as well.
But in this environment, it has access to more tools.
So like a data scientist, in addition to be able to answer quick questions like that, will want to do like more complex analysis.
So the agent has the ability to like.
Write Python for you and do more complex queries, types of things that like data scientists might do.
So if I typed in like, help me create a predictive model to see who our top selling enterprise rep will be in the next two quarters.
It can actually think through that like more complex analytical problem and actually like create code here in this notebook environment that will help me like build that model.
And, you know, obviously like we also have the tools here for the data scientists to go like go write Python themselves if they know how to do that.

But like one of the ways that AI is really helping with workflows like this is just making them like 10 times faster because the AI can just generate a first draft for you in seconds that might, you know, take someone an hour to create even.
And if they're an expert, and you can see, it even knows the Hex documentation, so it can be like, oh, what's the best way to do this analysis?

Well, here's the approach I would recommend, and I could maybe say, I want, maybe I'll just say, I want a time series regression model, and give it instructions and kind of have a conversation to kind of help direct it as it goes through the analysis.
And then as that's running, the last thing we do is once we have an analysis we like, we can turn that into an app.
So let's imagine what I've created here is exactly what I want to distribute to my non-technical users in my organization.
I want to turn this into like an app that I share with my whole sales team, and they're all tracking it to track their performance.
We can just publish this as a Hex app, and then that is now something I can just share with a link.
And people can go access the latest version of this report by sharing that link around.
And then they can use that as the jumping off point for additional analysis.
So that's kind of in a nutshell, like the workflow that a lot of people go through with Hex is for the business user.
They're in that threads environment, kind of doing these more simple questions.
The more data science users are in the notebook environment, kind of doing deeper analysis.
And then it kind of accumulates in these apps that then turn into these reusable kind of stores of answers for the organization.
Yeah.
And I notice Snowflake B2B was down there in the bottom right, kind of present when you start to search or type in your query.
Is the user able to select different data types or is it just the one that's available?
Yeah, Snowflake B2B is the data connection that I'm using.
But you can connect to like any data source.
So we have like.
Big Query connection here.
In fact, like if I go back into the kind of main screen, you can see that like we can connect to any data warehouse that the customers might have.

Usually our customers are like cloud customers, so they'll be on like Big Query, Snowflake, Databricks, AWS, Redshift, sometimes Microsoft Azure, but we even connect to some other types of data, maybe on-prem data, things like that.
The customer just like establishes a connection to their data warehouse, and then PECs can run queries against it.
Gotcha.
And what's that data connection like?
Is it kind of just SSO?
Is it integration?
So I think I can do that here.
Let's see.
I might not have permissions to.
Start a new one, but yeah, you basically select the data source that you're connecting to, and then you put in the connection.
If you're a Snowflake customer, you type in the URL where that Snowflake connection is.
You put in your credentials.
We support SSO through Okta, so if customers are enforcing security that way, but you can also, if you have data-based permissions, you can do it that way.
And then we just run queries directly against the warehouse.
Yeah.
I guess thinking of more of the kind of newer positioning with, you know, Hex is not just for data scientists.
Probably looking at maybe more of the early adopters of it, but what would you say like the user breakdown is typically for those types of companies that are really engaging their business users?
Like a 70-30 data team to business team?
Yeah, it's a good question.
think we're definitely still in the early stages.
We just released this Threads thing a month ago.
In the few customers that have started to adopt it, they reach economies of scale very quickly because a business team is such a large multiple of people on the data team.
At Hex, we have three data people in a 150-person company.
That's the case for a lot of our customers, too.
like, PandaDoc is a customer that is using Threads extensively.
Basically, what they did is they started with, like, the finance team.
And they were like, we want to provide everyone on the finance team with access to doing, like, quick questions through this Threads environment.
They set up some data connections and models for that team.
And then, like, now the full finance team is activated on it.
So, like, becomes a multiple of the data team very quickly.
Right now in most of our customers.
There's, I'd say it's still a majority like data people, but like as people adopt this Threads product, there will be more business users than data people.
Yeah.
How would you guys say you kind of fit into the broader data stacks?
So like, you know, you have connections with Snowflakes and Databricks and, you know, I've heard it's not like a, you don't have to rip and replace your Tableau instance.
Like it can play alongside it.
Yeah.
So what exactly does it replace outright versus what does it play nicely with?
Yeah.
Historically, we have played pretty nicely with like the Tableaus of the world because Hex does like much deeper analysis.
Like the notebook capability is really unlike anything at Tableau.
Tableau is more of the dashboard side.
side.
Tableau
And so customers would use like Tableau or Looker or something like that for dashboards.
And then they might use Hex for like really enabling like deeper data scientists and data analysts to like just do their own exploratory work without having to go through the dashboard tool.
Historically, that's been a really good fit for us.
With threads, we are kind of positioning ourselves more as a competitor to those BI tools.
And that is a majority of our opportunities now are competitive with those BI players.

It's a pretty different approach than like a Tableau or a similar tool where like that tool is kind of more around like building dashboards and we're kind of answering the same types of questions, but in these natural language threads, but the same type of user.
And so that's something we're trying to get good at is like, how do we position ourselves?
I guess how do you kind of want Hex to feel compared to those tools?
Is there kind of a voice or visual identity that you guys are going for?
Or what's the emotional tone of the brand against Tableau and Looker?
Yeah, I mean, I think like from a product side, we really believe like the AI, like being AI centric and being at the cutting edge of that is our path.
To winning, I think in terms of like a tone, that's more modern, more innovative, more challenging, sometimes like sort of, what's the right word?
Like sometimes a little bit like anti-establishment in some ways where we're like saying, you know, isn't it, isn't it crazy that you like have this?

Process with this BI tool where you have to have people build these reports and you send the reports to business people just to answer a simple question, like, shouldn't there be a better way than that?
So that's kind of the vibe we're trying to communicate.
Definitely feels that way just kind of from the simple demo you gave.
I think earlier we used Looker for simple dashboard setups and I would not be comfortable setting it up myself or even trying to query.
Yeah.
The data to get to that point.
So it definitely feels a lot more self-serve than these other tools that are allegedly, you know, business user friendly.
Yeah, that's what we hear from customers a lot is like they like the Lookers and the Tableaus and the Power BI's of the world have kind of like said that they're for business users.
But in practice, it really takes like a data analyst to kind of like build the stuff.
And so So

They're kind of just consumers, and then, like, they really quickly run into these situations where they do have, like, a question that wasn't, like, designed into the report, and then they're kind of stuck in this kind of QQ cycle where they're like, oh, I send the report, I send the request over to the data analyst, and then I wait a couple weeks, and then I get something, and then I have a new question, I start over.
Like, that's kind of the pain that we hear from a lot of our customers.

Yeah, I can imagine, like, the quick questions, it's like, A, it takes time for the data analyst to get to the question, and then it takes time for them to develop everything to answer the question, and then send it, versus this, it's much quicker.
So, is, like, speed to insight or speed to value something you guys have kind of cornered on?
Yeah, I love that concept.
We have heard that from a lot of our customers.
Pre-AI, because we have this workspace that combines a lot of query and data science and visualization in one tool.
Even that, for a lot of our customers, they feel a lot faster.
But I think even more now with the AI capabilities, you can just get answers faster is a great way to position.
Yeah.
I think somebody, I forget who specifically said it, but they said that you guys are the cursor of data.
Can you tell me a little bit more about what that means internally?
Is it about the experience?
Is it the AI aspect, speed, governance, all of the above?
Yeah, I think it's sort of an aspirational comment, but I think we're latching onto the fact that Cursor has completely transformed the workflow for developers.
People that develop software, again, they're just so much faster because they have Cursor create the first draft of code.
They have Cursor edit code for them.
They
It's made, it's completely transformed the way like most developers work.
And I think we like, we aspire to have the same impact for data people is like, you know, there's all these different tasks that data people have to do.
And we think a lot of those tasks can really be made so much faster with AI tools at their disposal.

I think also like just the trajectory cursor has had like, and the way they've built their brand is kind of like this new tool that like is running lean, but they've really like, they've penetrated the market.
So, well, I think is also like something that's inspirational to us.
So yeah, like if, if like data analyst customers were like, yeah, it's like cursor for me because that's, it like knows the, knows the tasks that I care about.
think that would be like a huge win for us.
Yeah.
Need to figure out what the tagline is like vibe data engineering or something.
Yeah, for sure.
I mean, like, um, um, um, um,
Yeah, Vibe data engineering.
Doesn't flow off the tongue quite as well as Vibe coding, but slightly nuanced.
So is the AI model that you guys are using, or is it custom-built, like homegrown, or is it a kind of guardrails version of one of the domain, like, main models?
Yeah, so right now we use Claude Sonnet as, like, the model.
I think it's Sonnet version 4.5.
And what we do to give it guardrails is we have a bunch of things in the product that provide, like, context in our prompt to the model.
So we have, I showed you during the Threads demo, we were, like, pulling, like, semantic definitions from the data.
So, like, you can either build or, like, import the semantic definitions into Hex.
And those are things like...
What's the table that has this information, and how does the metric compute it, and what does the table join to?
That provides instructions for the AI models, like just know where to find the data and how to compute certain things.
We also have like rules files in the product.
So like if there's certain ways an organization needs to do analysis, like, you know, if you're asking a question about revenue, we use this table.
Or like if this number is going up, it's good.
Like those types of things you can kind of define in Hex.
And then it also pulls, the data team can also like endorse certain content, being like this table is specifically useful for analysis.
Or this project is published, and it's like a good source of this type of information.
So we provide all that as context in the prompt that we send the LLM, and that provides like better, more accurate.

And then we have basically trained our prompts with certain tools, like how to control the Hex environment, like create a Python script or create a query or create one of those visualizations, like taught the LLM how to control those parts of our platform.
Gotcha.
Let's say like a user goes through that workflow that you showed me and they create an app.
What's the ability for them to embed that somewhere else or to share it outside of Hex?
Or is it mainly users come to Hex to see a kind of raw data analysis or summary?
Yeah, they can embed it.
We do have some customers that do that so they can like take the app or just a visualization or something and embed it in their own like web portal.
Some customers do it that way.
You know, organizations often like.
Have that Hex app be like, they'll share out the link to that app and they'll come in and they'll log in and then they'll view it there.
We also have a Slack integration.
So this is something that we just launched last week, but we have a couple of customers using it and we use it internally where you can just add Hex in like a Slack prompt.
And it uses the same technology behind the scenes.
You can link back in the Hex, but it can actually in a Slack thread, like present all those explanations and visualizations.
And we also have an MCP server.
So like you can kick off like a thread in chat GPT if your organization is using Hex.
Yeah.
For those like, no, MCPs, never mind.
I was just going to ask if it's more of like an official partnership you guys have with those, but MCP.
It's more like people set it up to use it.
Yeah, we do have some official partnerships.
So the ones we launched with were Claude and Cursor as like supported apps that you can just like the it's already configured and you just install it.
And then we're building where I think the next ones are like Notion and ChatGPT and then we'll build more.
But but it's it's an MCP, so you could like go deploy it anywhere that can use an MCP.
How does Hex deal with like unstructured or partially modeled data?
Is it pretty agnostic to unstructured and structured or is there one that does better?
It it can connect to.
to.
Structured data, to be able to analyze the data, we'll need some structure applied to it.
Our notebooks have the tool to do simple data transformations.

So if you had a semi-structured data that was concatenated or had free text and you wanted to parse out certain things and build that into a structure to then analyze, that can be done in our notebooks.
To be able to get the rich insights I was showing, you need structure for the data.
So a lot of times customers will do that in Hex, or they might do it outside of Hex.
They might use some data transformation tool and then connect Hex to that.
Gotcha.
You talked me through adoption.
Is there certain data that shows once people use Hex, they stick with it?
Or like expansion, like once a small team uses it, it starts to stay.
It's been multiple seats.
Yeah, I'm trying to think of a good data point.
I definitely have some data on it.
I'm just trying to think of the best thing to share.
mean, we consistently see, like high level, we'd consistently see our net revenue retention.
That's like a metric we pay attention to at like 160%.
So like for accounts that renew, they renew at 60% more than they originally paid, which shows like pretty healthy expansion on average.
I might have something more detailed I could track down and share with you.
That's good.
think just generally that stat speaks for itself.
Yeah.
How do you guys measure kind of accuracy or reliability through that, at least the threads experience?
Is there a way that kind of, you know.
I people now are using Chats, PT, and Claw.
looking at citations as like, okay, at least I can go see where the source came from.
And I know you showed a little bit of that of like pulled from this report or pulled from this app that already exists.
But there are other ways you guys kind of help re-insure accuracy and reliability.
Yeah, I'll pop back in and I'll show you kind of like where we do that.
I know I kind of showed it really quickly.
Let me just pull my screen back up.
So this is the thread I was showing before.
And yeah, like the first thing we do is we show like, okay, we searched these existing projects.
So those are like the apps that I showed.
And then we searched like these semantic models.
So those are like the data definitions.
And you can actually click through and like see the full like lineage of the data, like how we define that metric and stuff like that.
We searched for certain tables.
And then you can actually break open like any of the work we did here and see like what chart we created or what query we ran.
So like in the thread, we show that we like kind of show our work as we go.
The user can like vote like good or bad.
And so we track that data of like what, like if someone does that, like what are they, what were they searching for?
Was a good response, was a bad response?
What do they see?
Um, and then, um, like if, if a user like got this, uh, report and was like, I'm not, I'm not sure if this is accurate or something like that.
Like what our expectation, what they do is they will like share it with like a member of their data team.
And so they can click share and then they can, the data team can open it in that notebook mode, which is what I showed you before of like, oh, like what, what queries actually ran here.
And it it was just.
Yeah, mean, I think we think it's, like, crucial to being able to get accurate answers.

think, like, there were, before, like, before we built this and before, like, vendors were really, like, actively investing in, like, these production-grade tools for AI and data, there was sort of an effort toward, like, building an eval set for AI and data.
Like, similar to how, like, ChatGBT had eval sets when they were, like, building their models to, like, check, like, the accuracy of responses.
And, like, no one ever got, like, over, like, 40% accuracy.
Like, with, like, you know, there'd be an eval set of 300 different analytically-related questions.
And, like, I guess just data is such a broad and varied discipline.
There's so many different ways to ask questions.
Like, no one was able to get, like, their own proprietary model to, like, be even accurate, like, a majority of the time.
And the ways that we have found to be...
More accurate is with lots of context.
So we think that's kind of crucial.
Our recommendation to customers is it's really important for you to prepare these semantic models that have the data definitions in them.
A semantic model has, just to show you kind of what it looks like to us, it has metric definitions, and it has dimension names and fields, and then also has joins.
So metric definitions would be things like unique visitors and how do we compute that on our website.
And dimensions would be fields and descriptions of fields so that when the AI agent is like, someone types in something vague like, you know, how is traffic on our website?
Like having a field name like UUX traffic underscore ID seven, right?
Isn't like super helpful, but if you have a description in there, like that actually.
It's very instructive and the AI agents can find those things and find the right stuff.
And then what are related tables is another piece of it.
Analytical questions really quickly go jump between different topics and data areas.
So the LLMs need to know what other tables they can bring into the analysis to help answer questions.
So we think it's really important that customers model that out before they give people these search-based tools at large if they want accurate results.

Board of the Threads experience, if a user asks a question that maybe we don't have the data uploaded or it's just not 100% confident that they can answer that, is there a kind of reroute that happens?
Let me see if I can make something.
That it won't find.
How many bleep plurps do we have?
What it should do, we'll see if it works, is like, I can't find that.
Is there a table that that lives in that you want to tell me about?
Maybe you should talk to your data team about where that might be.
That's what we want the agent to do.
Yeah, so this is what it gives you.
I couldn't find any data.
Like, can you give me some more context?
I'm not really sure what you're looking for.
So that's actually how we want the agent to perform.
So you can't just type in, like, some random question.
Yeah.

And then for questions that do make sense, and you guys have the data for, is there, like, an encouragement to, like, follow on, or is it kind of like a full stop, like, here's the answer, you're good to go?
Yeah.
If we go back to my previous three.
I whoops, that's the wrong thing, I think where it left off was, here's a report that might be relevant.
Depending on the analysis, sometimes it'll be like another way you might analyze this is by looking across this segment or something like that.
Anyone can obviously continue the conversation by being like, is there a difference between this and this or something like that.
But this is where LMs are a little bit non-deterministic, so it's kind of based on what it finds.
But I have seen examples where it'll be like, here's a few, there's a few different ways to answer this question, you might try this approach.
Yeah.
I'm thinking about kind of when you guys are competing against, you know, maybe Omnit or Sigma.
What helps you guys win in those conversations?
Is it the AI native play?
Yeah, the AI native play definitely helps.
I think those vendors would even concede we're ahead of them on AI, and they'd try to win in some other way.
We see Sigma trying to win by just being like, we're more native on Snowflake.
We're built to run queries in Snowflake, and we're going to be faster.
But they will kind of concede that we're ahead on AI.
So if the customer really values AI, we're definitely at an advantage there.
Same thing with Omni.
I think those tools are a little bit more of an incremental build on the previous paradigm of dashboards.
They do have AI, but they're more like AI in the service of building dashboards.
And we're changing the interface a little more.
The other.
Another thing that I've seen be effective is like our ability to do advanced analytics, because BI tools are kind of limited in like, they can write SQL, but they can't write Python.
So they can do like aggregations and data transformations, but they can't like create an ML model.
And so like if the customer values that, then that's an advantage for us as well.
Yeah, I think in more longer term, is the goal to kind of replace BI or kind of just help redefine it?
Yeah, I mean, I guess like that's something we talk about a lot.
Like I think, I think we kind of see those things as maybe one and the same.
Like I was just as a, as a point of comparison, I was at Tableau for a long time.
I worked there for 14 years and like in the early days of Tableau, we were kind of seen as like redefining BI.
Like we were like, it's this sort of visual interactive environment is very different from like the traditional tools that.
We're kind of more like lists of numbers.
And like over time, we just became BI.
Like people were like, oh, this is the new standard.
And then we just were BI and people replaced their tools with us.
But that took a long time.
I think that similar trajectory is in front of us at Hex where it's like, we're currently seen as like a different, a re-imagining of BI.
But if we're wildly successful, like that will just be the new definition of BI.
And like all the tools will kind of consolidate around those capabilities.
Yeah.
I guess, where do you guys see yourself going next if this is kind of the redefining BI?
Is the interface changing?
Is it core proactive?
Is it role-based kind of context engineering?
Definitely investing in the context engineering thing.
We do think there's some, we think we kind of have the interface that we want with like this threads thing and like agentic notebooks and apps.
We think that's like the right paradigm.
We think there's some more ways we can make those like really accessible and easy for every user.
Like a thing that we think we'll build in the short term is a way to chat with your app.
So like, imagine you get that app that I built shared with you.
Like instead of going into a separate experience, you should just be able to like type a question right there and then like have the agent respond like in the context of your dashboard.

We also are really interested in this concept of, some people call it like ambient analytics of like stuff that's just kind of running in the background that can kind of like be proactively notifying you when you need to be paying attention to something.
So like you imagine that thread I typed of like who are our top selling rep.
Like, you can imagine me being like, responding to like, okay, follow this and update me once a week.
And now I just have like a bot that's running and like, I'm a sales manager and I can see who my top performing reps are, like, without having to go start a new thread every time.
So we're really interested in that type of stuff.
Yeah, it's almost as like, got your KPIs and triggers of like, if it hits a low, if it's high, it's within the threshold for the quarter.
Yeah, exactly.
And we have some of that in the product now.
Like, for example, we did a launch last month and you can configure Hex to be like, okay, let us know when we've got 5,000 unique views on this launch page.

But you can imagine that like, being really seamless with like, AI and chatbots and Slack where it's like, you don't even like the tool kind of disappears and you're just having a conversation with your data.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And it's showing up when you need it.
Yeah.
I think the term agentic analytics is starting to pop up more often.
I saw you guys post a blog post recently kind of defining it yourselves.
Yeah.
How do you guys feel about that term in general?
it kind of another buzzword or is it just buzzword for now but needs to be defined better?
I like the term.
The concern I have is like I just feel like it's so easy for every vendor to just copy stuff like that.
Like there's no reason any competitor of ours couldn't just like slap that on their web page.
And I don't know.
That's why like taglines and marketing terms are hard.
But it seems like it seems like it power.

Our full concept right now, I think we're just going to have to, like, be consistently vigilant about, like, what are the latest trends and how do we build, like, a brand identity that, like, kind of is bigger than just trying to attach to, like, a trend.
Yeah.
If you had to pick, like, one line for the market to associate with Hex kind of over the next six to 12 months, what would you think that might be?
Um, this probably isn't going to be, like, worded correctly, but I would say, like, Hex, um, Hex, like, brings the power of AI to data.
Um, something like that.
Yeah.
And then I think it was said in kickoff, like, users prefer show, not tell.
What would be the best way to show like Hex, like how should Hex show not tell that it's AI native?
That's a good question.
I don't know.

I mean, I think like one thing that comes to mind is like in the context of like very relevant like user workflows, like you could imagine like us kind of communicating what we do like in the context of like a growth marketer.
Like a growth marketer can just type you like have a, you know, a video or a motion graphic or something of like a search phrase that would be really relevant to a person in that role.
And then like showing what the experience would look like.
And maybe you could even do that like side by side, like old and new.
Like, the old way is, you know, file a ticket with the data team, you know, in a couple days, maybe you get a response back.
You get a screenshot that you paste into a PowerPoint deck, and then you go to your meeting, and then you ask a follow-up question, and you have to start over.
And new is type a prompt, get this live view that you, you know, ask in your meeting on the fly, and you answer the question, and then you make a decision, or something like that.
could see that being, like, a short vignette, or something like that.
I could even see that being done for, like, multiple types of roles.
There's one for a marketer, there's one for, like, a sales leader, there's one for, like, a product team member, there's one for, like, a people leader, or something like that.
I think that would be a really interesting way to see it come to life.
I mean, we do a lot of demos and put a lot of stuff online like that, just so people can, like, see the product in action.

Let's see, I guess when it comes to content quality, I think that's been a topic that was risen both as like a reason you guys parted ways with your previous agency, but also kind of your CEO's like, you know, content without soul is really no place with Hex.
So where does the content quality kind of break down today?
it technical level?
Is it just the speed and review cycles, speed bandwidth, or?
Yeah, I think it's, I think a lot of it is like style.
I think that like our CEO has like a pretty specific tone and style he wants to see in content.
And it's very high production quality.
Like if it's video, it's like commercial advertisement level quality.
It's not like a recording of a loom.
If it's written, it's like it definitely the expectation.
It needs to sound like someone who understands the product and needs to be kind of irreverent, needs to be kind of quirky and funny.
And it's a little bit like internet brained.
It's a little bit like this person's been on Reddit a lot.
Like it's sort of an innovative kind of style that he writes in.
I think he expects most stuff he sees to be in that style.
He would even say like, that's not that other styles are bad.
It's just like, that's the tone I want us to have.
So that's why a lot of stuff like kind of gets like rejected as like cringy or inauthentic.
of like this this is lot this a
Is there any specific formats that have performed really well historically, or at least kind of from your personal perspective?
I mean, we haven't, like, done that many different formats, to be honest with you.
We do a lot of blog, like, you know, a thousand-word blog.
We do a lot of, like, highly produced video.
Like, we'll do a launch.
We'll do, like, a little comedy sketch and a little product demo, and, like, looks great.
I, we, and then we do, like, we do social media stuff.
I don't know if that's been, like, a huge success for us, but we try to distribute that way.
We do a of email marketing.
I think probably, like, our most, like, the content pieces that we feel most proud of are probably, like, blogs that are, like, really thoughtful takes on a topic that kind of have, like, a thought.
We

And then for our content, given that we're trying to hit like five pieces per week once we get up to speed, and this is less product focus and more just conversation at this point, but have we, I guess, have you guys thought of like where that might live?

If it's maybe higher level, like top of funnel content that doesn't fit the kind of archetype for great content, not that we're not going to shoot for great content, but like if it is more of that top of funnel stuff, is it something that would live alongside the blog pieces or?
Well, I'd love to like, I think we'd like to hear a little better about like what, if that affects performance at all, like it with our previous vendor, we kind of hit it, like it was on.
We didn't link to it from anywhere, and I don't know if that had an impact on it not performing as well, but I think we're open to it.

Living in a section on the website, I've seen some vendors that have a special page that maybe they brand with a certain term, it's kind of around background information on the industry or terms or something like that.
But I've seen vendors completely hide it and just have it be findable by search engines.
So I don't think we have a vision for that, per se.
Pete might have some ideas, but I think we're open to ideas there.
Okay.
Yeah, because we've done it every way you can think of that you've mentioned, and I think we just want to...
We obviously want to hit the mark on quality, but also want to be...
Is it that scale and quality and speed are hard to kind of hit all three at the same time?
So more so just kind of trying to think ahead if there's something on the site that we may need to adjust to kind of have it be indexed, but and still rank on kind of the authority of the blog.
I think more for us to think through.
Nothing to solve today.
Okay.
Well, I'll make sure that we're thinking about it as well and see if we have an opinion there, but that's a good question.
Wonderful.
So that's most of the questions I had.
Anything I didn't ask that I should have asked?
Not that I can think of.
I mean, I'm always available for more questions.
If you think of anything later, you can shoot me a note.
So I appreciate you spending the time.
Yeah.
Let me know.
Let me know what else I can
Absolutely.
No, this has been super helpful so far.
So I appreciate the time and the insights.
And we'll be in touch through Slack or see you on the meeting next week.
All right.
You got it.
Thank you.
All right.
See you.
Thanks.
See you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Deep dive on Hex's AI-native product and market positioning.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=40.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Hex is redefining BI with an AI-native workflow.** It replaces the slow request-queue cycle with instant, trusted answers via natural language, enabling business users to self-serve and freeing data teams for advanced work.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=150.0)
  - [**The platform unifies the full data lifecycle:** natural language queries (`Threads`), advanced data science notebooks, and shareable low-code apps. This eliminates the need for multiple, disconnected tools.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=314.0)
  - [**Trust is built on transparency.** The AI's reasoning is fully auditable, showing all data sources, queries, and logic. This is enabled by customer-defined semantic models that provide essential business context.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1847.0)
  - [**The brand is modern and irreverent,** challenging the status quo of legacy BI tools. Content must be high-quality and align with a specific "internet-brained" tone set by the CEO.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1144.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: Legacy BI's Broken Workflow

  - [Legacy BI tools (Tableau, Looker) create a slow, frustrating request-queue cycle for business users.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=150.0)
  - [This forces data teams to spend time on one-off questions instead of high-impact projects.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=260.0)
  - [The root cause is a fragmented toolchain (querying, science, reporting) that prevents a unified, AI-native experience.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=224.0)

### The Solution: Hex's Unified, AI-Native Platform

  - [Hex solves this by unifying the data workflow in one platform with integrated AI.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=314.0)
  - [**1. Threads (Self-Service):**](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=387.0)
      - [Natural language interface for business users to ask questions.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=407.0)
      - [AI generates detailed reports with tables, charts, and explanations.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
      - [**Trust Mechanism:** The AI's reasoning is auditable, showing all sources (projects, semantic models, queries).](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1847.0)
      - [**Efficiency:** The AI first searches for existing answers to prevent duplicate work.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=541.0)
  - [**2. Notebooks (Deep Data Work):**](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=601.0)
      - [`Threads` analysis can be instantly transferred to a notebook for deep data science.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=601.0)
      - [The AI agent in notebooks can generate complex code (Python, SQL) for tasks like predictive modeling.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=648.0)
      - [This accelerates expert workflows by providing a high-quality first draft.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=706.0)
  - [**3. Apps (Shareable Insights):**](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=746.0)
      - [Notebooks can be published as low-code apps for easy distribution to non-technical users via a shareable link.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=746.0)

### The Technology: How Trust is Built

  - [Hex uses Claude Sonnet (v4.5) but ensures accuracy by providing it with rich, customer-defined context.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1454.0)
  - [**Semantic Models:** Define business logic for the AI:](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1489.0)
      - [Metric definitions (e.g., "Closed Won Conversion Rate").](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
      - [Dimension descriptions (e.g., "UUX\_traffic\_ID7" → "Unique Website Visitors").](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2139.0)
      - [Table joins and relationships.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2119.0)
  - [**Rules Files:** Enforce organizational standards (e.g., "for revenue questions, use this table").](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1515.0)
  - [**Endorsed Content:** Data teams can mark projects or tables as authoritative sources for the AI to prioritize.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1535.0)

### Market Positioning & Brand

  - [**Competitive Landscape:**](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1035.0)
      - [**Legacy BI (Tableau, Looker):** Hex is now a direct competitor, offering a fundamentally different, AI-native approach.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1035.0)
      - [**Modern BI (Omni, Sigma):** Hex's AI capabilities and advanced analytics (Python support) are key differentiators.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2330.0)
  - [**Brand Identity:**](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1144.0)
      - [**Tone:** Modern, innovative, irreverent, and "internet-brained."](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1144.0)
      - [**Positioning:** Challenges the status quo of legacy BI, framing it as an outdated process.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=1144.0)
  - [**Future Vision:**](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2498.0)
      - [**Chat with your App:** Ask questions directly within a published app.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2517.0)
      - [**Ambient Analytics:** Proactive, scheduled analysis (e.g., a weekly report on top reps).](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2565.0)
      - [**Agentic Analytics:** The AI agent will become even more powerful and autonomous.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2498.0)

### Content Strategy

  - [**Quality Mandate:** All content must be high-quality and align with the CEO's specific tone.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2888.0)
      - [**Video:** Commercial-grade production, not simple screen recordings.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2924.0)
      - [**Written:** Irreverent, quirky, and "internet-brained" style.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2924.0)
  - [**"Show, Don't Tell" Strategy:**](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2753.0)
      - [Create short vignettes showing the "old way" (request-queue) vs. the "new way" (instant AI answer).](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2753.0)
      - [Target specific user roles (e.g., growth marketer, sales leader).](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2753.0)
  - [**Content Placement:** Open to ideas for housing high-volume, top-of-funnel content (e.g., a separate section, SEO-only).](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=3061.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**GrowthX:** Develop content concepts demonstrating the "old way vs. new way" for specific user roles.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=2753.0)
  - [**Hex & GrowthX:** Discuss placement strategy for high-volume, top-of-funnel content.](https://fathom.video/share/xgp7aQDcgxf_MxuDnaMbzUb_ttzjFckC?tab=summary&timestamp=3061.0)

action_items.description: Decide top-of-funnel content placement; update Erik
